phrasal_verbs.meaning: suddenly become unconscious; make a place dark by turning off all the lights, blackout (n)
become conscious again after being unconscious; happen again; go to a place where someone is, to visit them; be persuaded to change your opinion or decision
begin to realise or understand something
laugh a lot, or make someone laugh a lot
check the health of someone who has received medical treatment; try to find out more about something follow-up (n)
make someone feel sad or lose hope
start to feel happy or well again after something bad has happened; solve or deal with a problem
try to hit or attack someone suddenly and violently; speak angrily to or against someone
die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone)
die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone)
cause difficulties or pain for someone; behave badly
manage to stay alive after you have been very ill or very badly injured; succeed in a very difficult situation, or help someone do this
become smaller and thinner and not look fresh and healthy; become weaker or smaller
manage to produce a quality or a reaction that helps you deal with a difficult situation
do something to prevent someone or something from harming you

idioms.meaning: waste time feeling upset about something bad that has happened and cannot be changed
suddenly become extremely angry without a good reason

idioms.idiom: give sb a taste/dose of their own medicine
idioms.meaning: treat someone in the same bad way they treat other people
decide not to do something that you usually do
accept a difficult situation without complaining or showing how you feel
used for telling someone not to get angry or upset
be very annoyed because you have made a mistake, missed an opportunity, etc
shout or do something that allows you to get rid of anger
leave a person or situation alone if they might cause you trouble

idioms.idiom: never/don't look a gift horse in the mouth
idioms.meaning: if you are given something good, you should not complain about it or try to find things that are wrong with it

B.instructions: Each of the words in bold, except one, is in the wrong place. Write the correct words on the lines.

Humans have to learn how to (10) ...... handle themselves within a society.

D.title: Circle the correct word or phrase
D.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

questions.sentence: If he's been bitten by a snake, we'll have to get him to a hospital to get the **vaccine / antidote** immediately.
They give most children a number of **vaccines / antidotes** these days to stop them getting serious diseases.
Have you been **inoculated / injected** against tetanus?
The symptoms are small red spots and slight skin **irritation / annoyance**.
I've cut my finger. Have you got **plaster / a plaster**?

My **diagnosis / prognosis** is that you're suffering from stress.
If you carry on taking the tablets, then your **diagnosis / prognosis** is excellent.

M.title: Word formation

N.title: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space
N.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of the line to form a word that fits in the space in the same line.
N.passage: There are many moral issues which remain (1) unresolved to this day. One of these is voluntary euthanasia. Let's say there is a patient who has a terminal disease. All possible treatments known to science are (2) ineffective. There's no cure, and no hope. The patient's death from this disease at some point in the future is (3) unavoidable. Most of us would say that, as long as the patient is fairly comfortable and pain-free, there's no moral issue. But what if the patient is in extreme agony? They cannot live (4) independently of machines and 24-hour care. What if the patient (5) resolutely and continually asks for their life to be brought to an end by doctors? Should the doctors refuse? At present, in most countries, there are laws against voluntary euthanasia. But from a moral point of view, we have to (6) acknowledge that there is a problem. Do the doctors really have the right to be (7) insensitive to the patient's wishes? On the other hand, opponents of voluntary euthanasia argue that scientific (8) knowledge is increasing all the time. An incurable disease one day may be curable the next. They also argue that if the law was changed, the (9) resulting situation would put doctors in a much worse position. They currently have no choice. Do we as a society have the right to expect doctors to (10) handle such a difficult and complex issue?

questions.sentence: moral issues which remain (1) ...... to this day. RESOLVE
All possible treatments known to science are (2) ...... . EFFECT
The patient's death from this disease at some point in the future is (3) ...... . AVOID
They cannot live (4) ...... of machines and 24-hour care. DEPEND
What if the patient (5) ...... and continually asks for their life to be brought to an end? RESOLVE
From a moral point of view, we have to (6) ...... that there is a problem. KNOWLEDGE
Do the doctors really have the right to be (7) ...... to the patient's wishes? SENSE
Opponents of voluntary euthanasia argue that scientific (8) ...... is increasing all the time. KNOW
If the law was changed, the (9) ...... situation would put doctors in a much worse position. RESULT
Do we have the right to expect doctors to (10) ...... such a difficult and complex issue? HAND
